title: A woman who never bleeds Dummy Plug as Tampon
display_title: A woman who never bleeds Dummy Plug as Tampon
wikitext:
[]: "A woman who never bleeds"; Dummy Plug as Tampon             Quote Post by Sirona » Mon Aug 05, 2013 9:24 am Ok, so the subject line is a joke, however I wanted to discuss further the idea that Rei doesn't menstruate, and some of the bits that I personally found interesting and related. I don't actually believe this theory can be proved, but I had fun considering a few things... First of all, the first time I watched the television series years ago, I always assumed that the line, "a woman who never bleeds", was Rei referring to herself, and didn't think much beyond that. Then, while browsing the wiki, I came across the following image: While it never occurred to me prior to viewing the wiki that Armisael is "bleeding" in this scene (particularly because there is no "menstrual blood" seen in the shot from the side of Armisael and Rei), I'm willing to play with the idea that menstrual bleeding was either overtly implied or at least symbolized by the shadow that appears to be blood. So, what if Rei is "a woman who never bleeds"? Even though clones would typically be able to procreate, what if something about Rei's creation renders her sterile? I also wonder if part of Armisael's "strategy" in communicating with her is to reflect back to her her deepest desires or consciousness. Armisael already displays a strategy of merging with and reflecting consciousness, as seen in the manifestation of the angel tower (which is how I tend to perceive that scene, reminiscent of Michael Crighton's Sphere ). What if Armisael is reflecting one of Rei's deep desires, to be a woman who bleeds, back to her? In general, and as a side note, I feel that the emergence of the angels and how they evolve throughout the series is indicative of a deeper theme regarding evolution within the Tree of Life, but that may be for a different thread. However, if that were the case, it would be natural for one of the last angels to be an angel of consciousness and reflective qualities. A couple of other things that aren't conclusive in any way, but that I found interesting... • Rei's embarrassment at being told by Shinji that she reminds him of "a mother". I find her reaction interesting, and it's not implausible that it is based on her knowledge that she can never be a mother. I'm aware that this detail in particular is inconclusive - the obvious alternative is that it simply brings up the idea of sex between her and Shinji. • The otherwise seemingly-random reference to Asuka's menstruation (perhaps opposite, as usual, to Rei) and how distasteful she finds it; and that Asuka mentions that she would never want to have children. Taken one step further, Rei's soul, being at least partially that of Lilith, the mother of all mankind, would possess the source of maternal nature itself. That combined with an inability to bleed or reproduce would create what could be considered a sacrilegious conflict. Perhaps part of the reason GNR seems so stoked in EOE when she's destroyed is that she is being sacrificed in order to bleed, which is essentially also "creating" her "children". Menstruation itself is essentially a creative act. One last thought, does anyone know whether Gendo is attempting to implant Adam into Rei's womb during the scene in EoE when she rejects him? Seems like he's moving his hand in that general direction... or maybe not so much an implantation as a detonation of 3I... I'm new to the board and thought this would be a fun idea to explore. If you want to "play" with this idea, I'd love to hear your thoughts!

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Mon Aug 05, 2013 1:59 pm I do like the idea that Rei's creation is effectively a "Forbidden Fruit" act that robs the Mother Goddess of her most fundamental attribute and dominion. I always considered it headcanon.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Aug 05, 2013 2:10 pm The nearest related thread I can see is this one from the archives : http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/6684/Rei-the-Moon-and-menstruation/ Tangentially related : http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/2124/So-is-LCL-intrinsically-primordial-soup-or-not/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/9293/Reis-Monologue/ http://forum.evageeks.org/post/525785/Eva-Units-and-Gender-References/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/3365/Lcl-Streak-Across-the-Night-Sky/ and probably a few more

[]: Re: "A woman who never bleeds"; Entry Plug as Tamp             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon Aug 05, 2013 3:17 pm Sirona wrote: I also wonder if part of Armisael's "strategy" in communicating with her is to reflect back to her her deepest desires or consciousness. Armisael already displays a strategy of merging with and reflecting consciousness, as seen in the manifestation of the angel tower (which is how I tend to perceive that scene, reminiscent of Michael Crighton's Sphere ). What if Armisael is reflecting one of Rei's deep desires, to be a woman who bleeds, back to her? In general, and as a side note, I feel that the emergence of the angels and how they evolve throughout the series is indicative of a deeper theme regarding evolution within the Tree of Life, but that may be for a different thread. However, if that were the case, it would be natural for one of the last angels to be an angel of consciousness and reflective qualities.

[]:             Quote Post by Sirona » Mon Aug 05, 2013 9:00 pm I do like the idea that Rei's creation is effectively a "Forbidden Fruit" act that robs the Mother Goddess of her most fundamental attribute and dominion.

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Tue Aug 06, 2013 1:38 pm I find the idea about procreative ability - that Rei wants what she can't have and Asuka doesn't want what she does have - to be valid. Rei is a neutered version of Lilith, and in a spiritual sense, an inert version of Yui. She has been deprived even the illusion of agency and her personality is forced to remain dormant, to the point where it is defined by its very dormancy. This is in contrast to Asuka, whose complex lies in an unsubstantiated delusion of power, ultimately an empty shell. Rei can't produce offspring and attain the power/status associated with mother(or father)hood that Yui did, so she can only be stuck as a pawn. Asuka doesn't want to use her means of production, because she is scared of the results it would bring. Here I am, strictly speaking, referring to the idea of procreation, but in this we see the grander, paradoxical mentality with with Asuka is stricken: She wants to lead, to be the best and have everyone love her, but at the same time she isn't too keen on the idea of taking the responsibility that attaining such a role requires. So her relation with Shinji is perpetually strained - she could be his friend, but she doesn't want to take the risk by keeping her end of the deal (akin to how a woman, in order to have a child, must give herself up to the pregnancy for a year, plus actually raise the thing). Instead, she fulfills her fantasy with a more specific one: that of Kaji as the proper man of her dreams. lol here I am talking about Asuka in a Rei thread. What's up with Rei, then? It's harder to say because Rei doesn't talk as much, but I'd say that her problem is more or less the same as Asuka's but coming from the direction of subdued inaction as opposed to futile, self-gratifying action. Asuka would be a viable protagonist but Rei couldn't be. In EoE, when Rei gets Adam stuck in her, I guess that could be seen as Gendo using her stolen agency of offspring-production for his own end (like a kind of rape? it's weird), which she then successfully rejects because she actually had power all along, she just didn't know or care about it. But the theme of power and submission here is interesting, because while Rei denies Gendo the power he thought he had, she does not keep it for herself - she gives it to Shinji. Her act of giving Shinji the keys to Instrumentality is similar Yui's act of giving birth to him in the first place. It is an act that at once implies the possession power as well as the loss of power, for the mother with a child is responsible to make some sacrifice for it. Maybe it's like the difference between potential and kinetic energy? In any case, Rei did what Asuka would not bring herself to accomplish - she placed her trust (and with it her love, I would say) in Shinji. This, I believe, was the fulfillment of her maternal wish.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Aug 06, 2013 4:38 pm The "Rei just wants to be normal" thing seems an obvious reading, but it's sharply in contrast to how I read her 'poem', specifically the line comparing people to flowers where she says they are all useless because they lack purpose. Yet it is her imposed special purpose which make her singled out and isolated, so which is the truth and which the rationalization? Does she want both but know they are incompatible? Speaking specifically of her motherliness in Shinji's eyes, it matters quite a bit to that scene whether or not she knows about her origins. I think it's a more nuanced issue and boiling it down to Rei just wanting to be normal is an oversimplification. She isn't some moe blob looking to be 'fixed' by the designated protagonist. It's also worth noting that menstuation signals the END of a reproductive cycle in which all that biologically expensive to maintain tissue is disposed of. This means that Rei might actually be CONSTANTLY fertile rather than infertile, or at least would be if perhaps she stopped taking her allotment of pills. The Lilith of Abrahamic mythology is essentially a personification on female sexuality unbound by the expected limits of god and man, afterall.

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Tue Aug 06, 2013 6:49 pm Here's another possibility: What if the "never bleeds" refers not to menstruation, but rather injury? Rei can be replaced, that's why she never truly "bleeds" as if she is killed another with the same memories comes to replace her.

[]: Re: "A woman who never bleeds"; Dummy Plug as Tamp             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Aug 06, 2013 8:51 pm I find Sirona's proposal of a reproductive "arc" in Rei's interactions and personality to be absolutely fascinating. This has big implications for Rei's relationship with, and later betrayal of Gendo, not to mention Gendo's appearance as Rei-II dies inside a now "pregnant" Eva-00. However, one point I have a problem with is the interpretation of the "Housewife" Elevator scene in #15. Sirona wrote: Rei's embarrassment at being told by Shinji that she reminds him of "a mother". I find her reaction interesting, and it's not implausible that it is based on her knowledge that she can never be a mother. I'm aware that this detail in particular is inconclusive - the obvious alternative is that it simply brings up the idea of sex between her and Shinji.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Aug 06, 2013 10:54 pm Note also that as Shinji talks about her being like a mother she squeezes liquid from a rag, in such a way that her forearms mimic the spreading of a woman's legs for intercourse; the final image of the twisted up rag facing the audience is reminiscent of a rose, held in front of her stomach.

[]:             Quote Post by Sirona » Wed Aug 07, 2013 11:53 am Her act of giving Shinji the keys to Instrumentality is similar Yui's act of giving birth to him in the first place. It is an act that at once implies the possession power as well as the loss of power, for the mother with a child is responsible to make some sacrifice for it. Maybe it's like the difference between potential and kinetic energy? In any case, Rei did what Asuka would not bring herself to accomplish - she placed her trust (and with it her love, I would say) in Shinji. This, I believe, was the fulfillment of her maternal wish.

Yet it is her imposed special purpose which make her singled out and isolated, so which is the truth and which the rationalization? Does she want both but know they are incompatible? Speaking specifically of her motherliness in Shinji's eyes, it matters quite a bit to that scene whether or not she knows about her origins. I think it's a more nuanced issue and boiling it down to Rei just wanting to be normal is an oversimplification. She isn't some moe blob looking to be 'fixed' by the designated protagonist.
